rulings.Case Title: ISRAEL ROSA VS. THE SFO FORECAST INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of And Unopposed Second Motion For Entry Of Dismissal Without Prejudice Of Plaintiffs Tenth Cause Of Action Under The Private Attorneys General Act And Dismissal Of Balance Of Action With Prejudice
rulings.Rulings: On the Law & Motion/Discovery calendar for Tuesday, April 28, 2026, Line 1, DEFENDANT THE SFO FORECAST INC.'S Notice Of And Unopposed Second Motion For Entry Of Dismissal Without Prejudice Of Plaintiffs Tenth Cause Of Action Under The Private Attorneys General Act And Dismissal Of Balance Of Action With Prejudice. Hearing required. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: ELIZABETH BLAND VS. MANUEL ALBERTO LOPEZ RAMIREZ ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Responses, Without Objections, To Defendant Allison Adel Goldenbergs Special Interrogatories (Set One); Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: On the Law & Motion/Discovery calendar for Tuesday, April 28, 2026, Line 2, 3. DEFENDANT MANUEL LOPEZ RAMIREZ'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Responses, Without Objections, To Defendant Allison Adel Goldenbergs Special Interrogatories (Set One); Request For Sanctions. Defendant Allison Adel Goldenberg's motion to compel responses to her special interrogatories, set one, from plaintiff Elizabeth Bland is granted. Bland is ordered to provide objection-free, verified responses by June 1, 2026. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 2030.290.) Bland asks for a continuance of the motion, but the discovery responses have been due since January 20, 2026. (Horton Decl., para. 2.) Defendant seeks sanctions. The court has granted sanctions on a companion motion and finds that Bland's discovery position lacks substantial justification but concludes that the amount awarded on the first motion is sufficient and does not award additional sanctions on this separate motion. Defendant shall give notice of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: ELIZABETH BLAND VS. MANUEL ALBERTO LOPEZ RAMIREZ ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Responses, Without Objections, To Defendant Manual Alberto Lopez Ramirezs Special Interrogatories (Set One); Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: On the Law & Motion/Discovery calendar for Tuesday, April 28, 2026, Line 2, 1. DEFENDANT MANUEL LOPEZ RAMIREZ'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Responses, Without Objections, To Defendant Manual Alberto Lopez Ramirezs Special Interrogatories (Set One); Request For Sanctions. Defendant Manuel Alberto Lopez Ramirez's motion to compel responses to his special interrogatories, set one, from plaintiff Elizabeth Bland, and for sanctions, is granted. Bland is ordered to provide objection-free, verified responses by June 1, 2026. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 2030.290.) Bland asks for a continuance of the motion pending her counsel's motion to withdraw, but the discovery responses have been due since January 20, 2026. (Horton Decl., para. 2.) Defendant seeks sanctions. Code of Civil Procedure, section 2030.290, subdivision (c) provides that "the court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a response to interrogatories . . . unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." Sanctions are appropriate on a discovery motion where the objecting party does not act with substantial justification in its discovery compliance, even where the party does not oppose the motion. (See Rule of Court 3.1348, subd. (a).) The court does not find substantial justification and orders Bland to pay sanctions of $1,372.50 to defendants Manuel Alberto Lopez Ramirez and Allison Adel Goldenberg within 30 days. Lopez Ramirez shall give notice of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And Consent Judgment As To Defendant Harmsco Inc.
rulings.Rulings: On the Law & Motion/Discovery calendar for Tuesday, April 28, 2026, Line 6, PLAINTIFF ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH ADVOCATES, INC.'S, Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And Consent Judgment As To Defendant Harmsco Inc. The court grants the unopposed motion to approve the Proposition 65 settlement and consent judgment. The court finds that the warning required by the settlement is in compliance with Proposition 65 (Health & Safety Code sections 25249.5 to 25249.14); that the award of attorney's fees pursuant to the settlement is reasonable under California law; and that, considering the factors set out in Health & Safety Code section 25249.7, subdivision (b)(2), and the facts asserted in the moving papers, the penalty amount is reasonable. Accordingly the motion is granted and the parties may submit the proposed judgment for signature. rulings.Case Title: T. F. VS. BLUE CROSS OF CALIFORNIA DBA ANTHEM BLUE CROSS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT BLUE CROSS OF CALIFORNIA DBA ANTHEM BLUE CROSS MOTION TO STRIKE AMENDED COMPLAINT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: On the Law & Motion/Discovery calendar for Tuesday, April 28, 2026, Line 8, DEFENDANT BLUE CROSS OF CALIFORNIA DBA ANTHEM BLUE CROSS MOTION TO STRIKE AMENDED COMPLAINT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY) (Part 2 of 2, tentative ruling continues from previous entry) Plaintiff T.F. alleges that Anthem breached its insurance contract with T.F. and breached the covenant of good faith and fair dealing in paying for care for T.F.'s minor son L.F. T.F. pleads facts showing that L.F. had grave mental health issues and needed specialized treatment. (FAC paras. 20-41.) L.F. was admitted to a residential treatment facility, which Anthem approved and found medically necessary but underpaid for according to its complicated and unexplained method of funding out-of-network treatments. (Id., paras. 46-49.) Anthem paid for about a month of treatment, and then refused payment for the next month on the basis of lack of medical necessity. Later it reversed its own determination and approved the additional month. (Id., paras. 56-59.) Anthem denied payment for treatment after that, and upheld its medical necessity denial. (Id., paras. 60-61.) T.F. alleges that Anthem's denial applied the wrong set of medical necessity standards and fell below generally accepted standards of care, as well as its obligations to provide parity in mental health and physical health coverage. (Id., paras. 62-78.) These factual allegations fail to provide specific facts giving rise to malice or oppression. T.F. alleges a dispute over coverage, acknowledges that Anthem reversed its initial medical necessity determination concerning the second month of treatment, and acknowledges that Anthem used a set of guidelines in making its decision, although T.F. disagrees as to which set of guidelines was appropriate. These facts do not rise to the level of malice or oppression. The court grants leave to amend. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. rulings.Case Title: T. F. VS. BLUE CROSS OF CALIFORNIA DBA ANTHEM BLUE CROSS ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On the Law & Motion/Discovery calendar for Tuesday, April 28, 2026, Line 8, DEFENDANT BLUE CROSS OF CALIFORNIA DBA ANTHEM BLUE CROSS MOTION TO STRIKE AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Blue Cross of California d/b/a Anthem Blue Cross's motion to strike the punitive damages allegations from the First Amended Complaint (FAC) is granted with leave to amend. The request for judicial notice is granted in part and denied in part. The court grants judicial notice as to Exhibits 1-3, the insurance policy at issue and denial letters referenced in the FAC. While the court takes judicial notice of the existence of these documents, it does not interpret them. (See Fremont Indemnity Co. v. Fremont General Corp. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 97, 113 ["Although the existence of a document may be judicially noticeable, the truth of statements contained in the document and its proper interpretation are not subject to judicial notice if those matters are reasonably disputable."].) The court denies judicial notice of the CASII guidelines; the contents of the CASII guidelines are not relevant to this motion, and the court cannot purport to apply the guidelines here. Turning to the motion to strike, this is a pleadings motion and the court assumes the truth of the facts stated in the FAC. "In order to survive a motion to strike an allegation of punitive damages, the ultimate facts showing an entitlement to such relief must be pled by a plaintiff. In passing on the correctness of a ruling on a motion to strike, judges read allegations of a pleading subject to a motion to strike as a whole, all parts in their context, and assume their truth." (Clauson v. Superior Court (1998) 67 Cal.App.4th 1253, 1255 [citation simplified].) "In addition to the requirement that the operative complaint set forth the elements as stated in section 3294, it must include specific factual allegations showing that defendant's conduct was oppressive, fraudulent, or malicious to support a claim for punitive damages. Punitive damages may not be pleaded generally." (Today's IV, Inc. v. Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority (2022) 83 Cal.App.5th 1137, 1193 [citation simplified].) "In the context of failure to properly administer an insurance claim, punitive damages are ordinarily based on 'malice' or 'oppression,' rather than on the third possible ground for the award, 'fraud.' Both 'malice' and 'oppression' are defined in Civil Code section 3294 as involving 'despicable conduct,' which in the case of malice 'is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others,' and as to oppression is 'conduct that subjects a person to cruel and unjust hardship in conscious disregard of that person's rights.' " (Bartel v. Chicago Title Ins. Co. (2025) 111 Cal.App.5th 655, 706 [citation simplified].) "Applying these standards, courts have held that punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's acts are reprehensible, fraudulent or in blatant violation of law or policy.... Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. As such, simple breach of contract, no matter how willful and hence tortious, is not a ground for punitive damages. Such damages are accessible only upon a showing that the defendant acted with the intent to vex, injure, or annoy." (Id., at p. 707 [citation simplified].) (Part 1 of 2, tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 2)
